content.text: Production agentic AI systems generate a distinctive mix of signals that conventional application monitoring does not capture: LLM latency distributions with long tails, token economics that directly translate to cost, tool-execution failure cascades, and multi-step reasoning loops whose depth varies per task. Metrics and dashboards make all of these observable in real time, enabling engineers to detect degradation, control spend, and debug failures in running agents.

Metrics are numeric measurements collected at regular intervals or on every significant event. For an agentic AI system, relevant metrics span at least four layers: LLM API interactions (latency, token counts, cost), tool executions (call counts, success/failure), agent control flow (loop iterations, plan steps, task duration), and business outcomes (task completion rate, user satisfaction, error escalation rate).

A dashboard is a curated collection of time-series panels, histograms, heatmaps, and stat tiles that make those metrics intelligible at a glance. In the context of AgentOps, a good dashboard lets an on-call engineer answer the following questions within 30 seconds: Is the system healthy right now? Is it costing more than expected? Which model or tool is responsible for elevated errors? Where in the agent loop are tasks stalling?
Together, metrics and dashboards form one pillar of observability — alongside distributed traces (
path did this specific request take?) and structured logs (
what exactly happened at each step?
). The three pillars are complementary: metrics surface that something is wrong, traces show where, and logs reveal why.

Agentic systems fail silently in ways conventional apps do not. An agent that reaches the maximum context window quietly truncates its history. A tool that returns malformed JSON causes the agent to retry until the per-task budget is exhausted. An LLM provider experiencing elevated latency slows every task without returning a 5xx error. Without metrics, these situations appear as vague user complaints days after they begin.
Token cost is another dimension that requires active monitoring. A production agent calling

with a 50,000-token system prompt on every turn will accumulate costs that are invisible to standard server metrics. Prompt caching effectiveness, token budget adherence, and model tier usage must all be tracked as first-class metrics or finance surprises accumulate over days rather than seconds.

Metrics also drive improvement. Tracking task completion rate by topic or user segment reveals which agent capabilities need better tool coverage or more robust prompting. Tracking p95 LLM latency by model and region enables informed routing decisions. Tracking loop depth identifies tasks where the agent is spinning without converging. None of these improvements can be made confidently without the data that a metrics pipeline provides.
Metric alerts fire within seconds of an error rate spike or latency regression, long before users file support tickets or dashboards are manually reviewed.
Per-task and per-model token consumption metrics enable accurate cost attribution per feature, team, or customer. Budget guardrails can be enforced programmatically before costs exceed thresholds.
When a specific task fails, correlated metrics — which tool was called, how many loop iterations ran, what the token count was — narrow the search space for root cause dramatically.
Historical metrics establish what normal looks like. Canary deployments and prompt changes can be evaluated against baselines with statistical confidence rather than gut feel.
Trends in requests per second, concurrency, and queue depth give engineering and infrastructure teams advance warning before resource ceilings are hit.
Metrics on task completion latency and error rate feed SLO dashboards directly, making it possible to produce objective uptime reports for internal or external SLAs.

or histogram observation is a small CPU and memory cost. In latency-sensitive, high-throughput agents this cost can become measurable, requiring sampling strategies that reduce data volume at the expense of completeness.
Metrics require cardinality discipline. A label dimension such as
added to a metric creates one time series per unique value. With 100,000 users, a single metric with a
label explodes into 100,000 time series, which can overwhelm Prometheus and inflate storage costs by orders of magnitude. High-cardinality dimensions belong in traces and logs, not in metrics.
Token counts and latency vary even for identical prompts. Metrics tell you that latency rose; they cannot tell you whether the model changed its reasoning strategy.
A task can complete in 500 ms with zero errors and still produce a factually wrong or unhelpful answer. Quality assessment requires evaluation harnesses, not metrics pipelines.

A poorly tuned alerting configuration fires too many alerts on noise, desensitizing on-call engineers to real incidents. Effective alerting requires careful threshold calibration and multi-window burn-rate rules.

At low traffic volumes — a new feature with ten users per day — metric time series are too sparse for meaningful statistical analysis. Minimum traffic thresholds are often required before alerts are trustworthy.
The observability pipeline for an agentic AI system operates in five stages: instrumentation, collection, storage, querying, and visualization/alerting.
Instrumentation is performed inside the agent code itself. The OpenTelemetry SDK provides a vendor-neutral API:

instruments record events as they occur. A counter increments monotonically — suitable for tracking the total number of LLM calls or tool invocations. A histogram records individual observations in configurable buckets and computes percentiles — suitable for latency and token-count distributions. A gauge holds the most recent value — suitable for tracking active concurrent agent tasks.

The OpenTelemetry Collector receives telemetry via OTLP (OpenTelemetry Protocol) over gRPC or HTTP. It applies processors — batching, attribute filtering, sampling — then exports to one or more backends. Metrics go to Prometheus or a compatible remote-write endpoint. Traces go to Jaeger or Grafana Tempo. Logs go to Loki. This fan-out from a single collector means the agent code is decoupled from the choice of backends.
Prometheus scrapes or accepts pushed metrics and stores them as time series identified by metric name plus a label set. A query such as

computes the per-second call rate for a specific model over the past five minutes. Grafana queries Prometheus, Tempo, and Loki simultaneously, correlating signals across the three pillars in a single dashboard. Alertmanager consumes Prometheus alert rules and routes notifications to PagerDuty, Slack, or email based on severity and on-call schedule.

The key metric types for agentic AI systems are: LLM call duration (histogram, bucketed at 100 ms, 500 ms, 1 s, 2 s, 5 s, 10 s, 30 s), token counts (histograms for input, output, and total tokens), cost per call (gauge or counter computed from token price tables), tool call counts (counter labelled by tool name), tool error rate (counter pair: total vs. failed), task completion status (counter with labels:
), agent loop depth (histogram of iterations per task), and active sessions (gauge).

attrs.alt: AgentOps observability pipeline: Agent System emits OTLP telemetry to the OTel Collector, which routes metrics to Prometheus, traces to Jaeger/Tempo, and logs to Loki. Prometheus drives Alertmanager for alert routing. Grafana provides a single-pane-of-glass dashboard querying all three backends. Alerts and dashboards flow to on-call engineers.

content.text: The diagram shows the full pipeline from left to right. The
emits OTLP telemetry — counters, histograms, spans, and structured log records — on every LLM call, tool execution, loop iteration, and task event. The
receives this data, batches and filters it, then fans out to three backends:
for structured logs. Prometheus feeds its alert rules into
, which routes notifications to PagerDuty or Slack.
queries all three backends and presents them as a single dashboard. On-call engineers interact with both Grafana and Alertmanager notifications to triage, debug, and improve the agent system.

Metrics and dashboards should be in place before a production agent handles real user traffic. Waiting until the first incident to set up observability means the first incident will last much longer than it needs to, and its root cause may never be fully understood. Even a single-developer side project with ten daily users benefits from a basic dashboard that shows error rate and token cost.
the agent makes LLM API calls — latency and token cost are unpredictable and must be measured to be managed.
the agent uses external tools — each tool is a failure point and must be independently tracked for error rate and latency.
the system handles more than a few dozen requests per day, when multiple team members need visibility, or when a contractual SLA exists.
instrumentation overhead becomes measurable on the critical path. Apply head-based sampling to traces and use histogram approximations rather than recording every individual latency value.
Do not rely solely on metrics when
debugging a specific failed task — combine metrics with trace drill-down and log search to reconstruct the full execution path.
Install the OpenTelemetry SDK and set up a meter provider pointing at the OTel Collector or a Prometheus pushgateway endpoint.
Define your metric instruments once at module level: a counter for LLM call totals, a histogram for LLM call duration, a histogram for token counts, and a gauge for active sessions. Label each with
— but not with high-cardinality fields like
Wrap every LLM API call in an instrumented context: record start time before the call, record end time and status after, observe duration and token counts in the appropriate histograms.
Wrap every tool call similarly. Increment a counter on each invocation labelled by tool name and status. Observe tool execution latency in a histogram.
Track agent loop depth by incrementing a counter per iteration and observing the final count at task end in a histogram.
Deploy the OTel Collector (either as a sidecar or a shared cluster service). Configure it to receive OTLP gRPC on port 4317 and export to Prometheus remote-write and to a trace backend.
Deploy Prometheus with a retention period appropriate for your on-call needs (30 days is a common default). Configure scrape intervals of 15 s for fast-moving agent metrics.

Import or build a Grafana dashboard with at minimum four rows: Overview (request rate, error rate, p95 latency, cost/hour), LLM Performance (TTFT, token distributions, model breakdown), Tool Health (per-tool error rate and latency), and Agent Efficiency (loop depth, task completion rate, active sessions).

Define Prometheus alert rules for the conditions that matter most: error rate above 5% for 5 minutes, p95 latency above 30 s for 10 minutes, token cost rate above 2x baseline, active session count at zero for 5 minutes (indicating a worker crash).
Route alerts through Alertmanager to the appropriate notification channel (PagerDuty for critical, Slack for warning) with severity labels and runbook links embedded in alert annotations.

# pip install opentelemetry-sdk opentelemetry-exporter-otlp-proto-grpc anthropic

import time
import anthropic
from opentelemetry import metrics
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PeriodicExportingMetricReader
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.metric_exporter import OTLPMetricExporter

# --- one-time SDK setup (call at process startup) ---
def setup_metrics(otlp_endpoint: str = "http://localhost:4317") -> None:
    exporter = OTLPMetricExporter(endpoint=otlp_endpoint, insecure=True)
    reader = PeriodicExportingMetricReader(exporter, export_interval_millis=15_000)
    provider = MeterProvider(metric_readers=[reader])
    metrics.set_meter_provider(provider)

setup_metrics()
meter = metrics.get_meter("agentic_ai", version="1.0")

# --- define instruments once at module level ---
llm_calls_total = meter.create_counter(
    "agent.llm.calls.total",
    unit="{call}",
    description="Total LLM API calls, labelled by model and status",
)
llm_duration = meter.create_histogram(
    "agent.llm.duration.seconds",
    unit="s",
    description="LLM call wall-clock duration (seconds)",
)
llm_input_tokens = meter.create_histogram(
    "agent.llm.tokens.input",
    unit="{token}",
    description="Input token count per LLM call",
)
llm_output_tokens = meter.create_histogram(
    "agent.llm.tokens.output",
    unit="{token}",
    description="Output token count per LLM call",
)
tool_calls_total = meter.create_counter(
    "agent.tool.calls.total",
    unit="{call}",
    description="Total tool executions, labelled by tool_name and status",
)
tool_duration = meter.create_histogram(
    "agent.tool.duration.seconds",
    unit="s",
    description="Tool execution duration",
)
loop_depth = meter.create_histogram(
    "agent.loop.depth",
    unit="{step}",
    description="Number of agent loop iterations per completed task",
)
active_sessions = meter.create_up_down_counter(
    "agent.sessions.active",
    unit="{session}",
    description="Number of concurrently running agent sessions",
)

# --- instrumented LLM call wrapper ---
def call_llm(
    client: anthropic.Anthropic,
    messages: list[dict],
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-5",
    max_tokens: int = 4096,
) -> anthropic.types.Message:
    attrs = {"model": model}
    start = time.monotonic()
    try:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            messages=messages,
        )
        attrs["status"] = "success"
        llm_input_tokens.record(response.usage.input_tokens, attrs)
        llm_output_tokens.record(response.usage.output_tokens, attrs)
        return response
    except Exception as exc:
        attrs["status"] = "error"
        attrs["error_type"] = type(exc).__name__
        raise
    finally:
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
        llm_calls_total.add(1, attrs)
        llm_duration.record(elapsed, attrs)

# --- instrumented tool wrapper ---
def call_tool(tool_name: str, fn, *args, **kwargs):
    attrs = {"tool_name": tool_name}
    start = time.monotonic()
    try:
        result = fn(*args, **kwargs)
        attrs["status"] = "success"
        return result
    except Exception:
        attrs["status"] = "error"
        raise
    finally:
        tool_calls_total.add(1, attrs)
        tool_duration.record(time.monotonic() - start, attrs)
Building the Full Agent Loop with Metrics

# A minimal agentic loop that emits metrics on every meaningful event
import anthropic

CLIENT = anthropic.Anthropic()
TOOLS = [
    {
        "name": "web_search",
        "description": "Search the web for recent information.",
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
            "required": ["query"],
        },
    }
]

def web_search(query: str) -> str:
    # Stub — real implementation would call a search API
    return f"Results for: {query}"

def run_agent(user_query: str, agent_id: str = "default") -> str:
    active_sessions.add(1, {"agent_id": agent_id})
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_query}]
    step_count = 0

    try:
        while True:
            step_count += 1
            if step_count > 20:          # hard cap prevents infinite loops
                raise RuntimeError("Agent loop exceeded maximum steps")

            response = call_llm(CLIENT, messages, model="claude-sonnet-4-5")

            # Extract text and tool-use blocks
            tool_blocks = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"]
            text_blocks = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "text"]

            if response.stop_reason == "end_turn" or not tool_blocks:
                # Task complete
                loop_depth.record(step_count, {"agent_id": agent_id, "status": "success"})
                return text_blocks[0].text if text_blocks else ""

            # Append assistant turn
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})

            # Execute tool calls and collect results
            tool_results = []
            for block in tool_blocks:
                result = call_tool(block.name, web_search, **block.input)
                tool_results.append({
                    "type": "tool_result",
                    "tool_use_id": block.id,
                    "content": result,
                })

            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

    except Exception:
        loop_depth.record(step_count, {"agent_id": agent_id, "status": "error"})
        raise
    finally:
        active_sessions.add(-1, {"agent_id": agent_id})

if __name__ == "__main__":
    answer = run_agent("What are the latest updates to OpenTelemetry?")
    print(answer)
Prometheus Alert Rules for Agentic AI

# prometheus/rules/agentic_ai.yaml
groups:
  - name: agentic_ai
    interval: 30s
    rules:
      # Error rate alert — fires if >5% of LLM calls fail over 5 min
      - alert: AgentLLMHighErrorRate
        expr: |
          (
            rate(agent_llm_calls_total{status="error"}[5m])
            /
            rate(agent_llm_calls_total[5m])
          ) > 0.05
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "High LLM error rate ({{ $value | humanizePercentage }})"
          runbook_url: "https://wiki.internal/agentops/runbooks/llm-errors"

      # Latency alert — fires if p95 LLM duration exceeds 30 s
      - alert: AgentLLMHighLatency
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(agent_llm_duration_seconds_bucket[10m]))
          > 30
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "LLM p95 latency is {{ $value | humanizeDuration }}"
          runbook_url: "https://wiki.internal/agentops/runbooks/latency"

      # Session drain — fires if no active sessions for 5 min (worker crash)
      - alert: AgentNoActiveSessions
        expr: agent_sessions_active == 0
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Agent worker appears to be down — no active sessions"

      # Cost burn rate — fires if token rate is 2x the 24 h average
      - alert: AgentHighTokenBurnRate
        expr: |
          rate(agent_llm_tokens_output_sum[15m])
          > 2 * avg_over_time(rate(agent_llm_tokens_output_sum[15m])[24h:1h])
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Token output rate is 2x the 24-hour average — possible runaway loop"

Consider a customer support agent that handles 2,000 inbound tickets per day. Each ticket may require one to five LLM calls and up to three tool calls (CRM lookup, knowledge-base search, ticket history fetch). The team runs a Grafana dashboard with six panels on the main screen visible to the on-call rotation:
— heatmap over time; the team noticed that p99 routinely spiked to 45 s on Tuesdays, tracing it to a scheduled batch job that competed for GPU capacity on the LLM provider's regional endpoint.
— computed from token histograms and a recorded-rules price table, refreshed every 30 s.
Tool error rate by tool name
— showed that the CRM lookup tool had a 12% error rate on weekends because the CRM ran batch maintenance jobs that throttled the API.
— surfaced that roughly 0.5% of tickets caused the agent to spin at 15+ iterations, consistently linked to ambiguous ticket descriptions without enough context for the agent to resolve.

Each of these insights led to a concrete improvement: routing Tuesday traffic to a different provider region, adding a CRM retry-with-backoff on weekends, and injecting a clarifying question into the agent prompt when the CRM lookup returned no results. None of these fixes would have been made with confidence — or discovered systematically — without the metrics and dashboards.

# Example: Prometheus recording rule that computes USD cost per hour
# (add to prometheus/rules/agentic_ai.yaml under the same group)

# Recording rules are evaluated periodically and stored as new time series.
# They are faster to query in Grafana than raw histogram operations.

# recording_rules:
#   - record: agent:llm_cost_usd_per_hour
#     expr: |
#       (
#         rate(agent_llm_tokens_input_sum[5m]) * 3600 * 0.000003   # $3 per 1M input tokens
#         +
#         rate(agent_llm_tokens_output_sum[5m]) * 3600 * 0.000015  # $15 per 1M output tokens
#       )

# In Grafana, reference the recording rule by name:
# agent:llm_cost_usd_per_hour{model="claude-sonnet-4-5"}

# Python equivalent for computing estimated cost in application code:
def estimate_cost_usd(
    input_tokens: int,
    output_tokens: int,
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-5",
) -> float:
    # Prices are approximate and vary by tier; always verify against provider docs.
    prices = {
        "claude-sonnet-4-5": {"input": 3.0e-6, "output": 15.0e-6},
        "claude-haiku-4-5": {"input": 0.8e-6, "output": 4.0e-6},
    }
    p = prices.get(model, {"input": 3.0e-6, "output": 15.0e-6})
    return input_tokens * p["input"] + output_tokens * p["output"]
Think of metrics and dashboards as the

. A pilot flying by visual reference alone can navigate on clear days, but in cloud, darkness, or an emergency, the only reliable information comes from the instrument panel: airspeed, altitude, heading, fuel remaining. The pilot does not watch the engines burn fuel — the fuel gauge does that continuously. The pilot does not time each turn manually — the turn coordinator does.

An agentic AI system in production is always flying in some degree of cloud. The LLM's internals are opaque. Tool calls may fail silently. Costs accumulate without any visible counter. Metrics instruments are the gauges, counters, and indicators that make the invisible legible. The dashboard is the cockpit: a curated arrangement of those instruments designed for the specific information needs of whoever is responsible for keeping the system in flight.

Just as a pilot would not remove the fuel gauge to save weight, an engineer should not ship an agent to production without its metrics. And just as an aircraft can have too many warning lights that a pilot learns to ignore, an agent dashboard that alerts on too many non-actionable conditions creates noise that masks real emergencies.

as metric labels creates one time series per unique value. A system with 100,000 users generates 100,000 Prometheus series for a single counter. Prometheus stores label sets in memory; large cardinality inflates RAM usage by gigabytes and makes queries slow. Keep metric labels to low-cardinality dimensions:
(where there are fewer than ~1,000 distinct agents). Store user and task identifiers in traces and logs, not metrics.
A common pattern is to record latency only when the LLM call succeeds. This produces a histogram that excludes the slowest requests — precisely the ones that matter most. Always record latency in a
block so that timed-out and errored calls are included in the distribution. Use a
label to distinguish success from error, not a separate code path that skips the histogram.
Alert Thresholds Set Once, Never Revisited

Alarm thresholds calibrated at launch quickly become stale. As traffic grows, baseline latency and error rates shift. As the model improves, token counts may shrink. Static thresholds that made sense at 100 requests/day fire constantly at 10,000 requests/day (false positives) or miss real degradations that stay below the original threshold. Review alert thresholds quarterly and whenever traffic patterns change significantly. Use multi-window burn-rate alerts (short window + long window) rather than raw threshold crossing to reduce false positives.

Computing cost estimates in Prometheus recording rules from token counts is useful for dashboards, but is not a substitute for verifying actual invoices against the provider. Token prices change, caching discounts apply, and batching APIs have different pricing tiers. The metrics-based cost estimate should be treated as a fast approximation for anomaly detection, not as authoritative billing data.
Record latency in a finally block.
Always capture the full wall-clock time including errors and timeouts. Errors are often the slowest requests, and excluding them hides the most painful user experiences.
Use histogram buckets matched to expected ranges.

LLM latency spans 100 ms to 120 s — use buckets at 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 30, 60, 120. Default SDK buckets (designed for HTTP servers) cluster in the 0–10 ms range and miss all the interesting LLM percentiles.
Define metric instruments once at module level.
Creating meters inside function bodies causes duplicate registration errors and leaks resources. Instantiate all counters and histograms at import time.

Maintain two dashboards: an operations dashboard for on-call engineers (error rates, latency, active sessions, alerts) and a product dashboard for the team (task completion rate, cost per task, model usage share). One dashboard trying to serve both audiences ends up serving neither.
Use recording rules for expensive queries.

PromQL expressions that compute p95 across a 24-hour window over millions of samples are expensive to evaluate interactively. Pre-compute them as recording rules that run on a 1-minute schedule so Grafana panels load in milliseconds.
When the meaning of a metric changes — for example, when

is redefined to include only non-cached calls — rename the metric or add a version suffix rather than silently changing semantics. Dashboard panels depend on metric names and break if the underlying meaning shifts.

Add a unit test that runs the agent against mock LLM responses and then inspects the in-memory metric reader to assert that counters were incremented and histograms were recorded. This prevents instrumentation from being accidentally removed during refactoring.

The OpenTelemetry SDK is designed for minimal hot-path overhead. Counter increments and histogram observations are lock-free in-memory operations that take roughly 100–500 ns per call. For an agent making 10 LLM calls per second, the instrumentation overhead is well under 0.01% of wall-clock time. The network export to the OTel Collector is asynchronous and batched every 15 seconds by the
, so it does not block the agent's request path.

Prometheus scales horizontally through federation and remote-write. At high cardinality — thousands of agent instances each with dozens of metric series — a single Prometheus server can ingest and query millions of time series. For very large deployments, Thanos or VictoriaMetrics can provide long-term storage, global query, and multi-region replication without changing the agent's instrumentation code.

15 s is appropriate for agentic systems where latency and error rate need to be detected quickly. 60 s is sufficient for cost metrics. Using 1 s scrape intervals for Prometheus on high-traffic systems inflates storage and scrape overhead without meaningful benefit.

At 1,000 tasks/minute, storing every trace becomes expensive. Apply head-based sampling at 10%–20% for normal traffic, with a rule that samples 100% of failed tasks and any task with loop depth > 10. This preserves all anomalous traces while controlling storage costs.

Structured logs should be written at INFO level for task start/end/error and at DEBUG for every loop iteration. In production, set the log level to INFO to reduce Loki ingest volume by 10–50x compared to DEBUG.

Dashboards that auto-refresh every 5 seconds with complex PromQL expressions generate significant query load on Prometheus. Set dashboard refresh to 30 s for operational dashboards and use recording rules to pre-compute expensive aggregations.
Vendor-neutral API; Python, JS, Go, Java; auto-instrumentation for HTTP clients
Pluggable receivers, processors, exporters; tail-based sampling; attribute filtering
PromQL for histograms and rates; Alertmanager integration; recording rules for cost computation
Multi-datasource panels; correlated metrics + traces + logs in one view; alert state history
Object-storage backed; deep integration with Grafana dashboards; trace-to-logs correlation
Label-indexed logs; LogQL; label set matches Prometheus for consistent filtering
Prompt versioning, LLM trace UI, token cost dashboard, eval integration — purpose-built for LLM apps
LLM Observability product; OOTB dashboards for OpenAI and Anthropic calls; integrates with APM traces
Teams that want fast time-to-insight on LLM calls
Metric data can inadvertently contain sensitive information when labels are constructed from user inputs. An attribute such as

attached to a span would embed raw user content in the observability backend, where it may be retained far longer than the primary application data and may be visible to a wider set of personnel. The OTel Collector's attribute processor can strip sensitive fields before data leaves the agent host.

Dashboard access control matters. A Grafana instance that exposes cost metrics to all company employees leaks competitive information about which AI features are being used and at what scale. Use Grafana's organization and folder permissions to restrict cost and performance dashboards to the engineering team, and provide product-level (non-cost) metrics to broader stakeholders.
OTel Collector as a security boundary.
Route all telemetry through the Collector before it reaches external backends. Configure the Collector to redact PII fields using the
processor, strip internal hostnames, and enforce data residency by routing to region-local backends.

Alertmanager is a dependency of the on-call workflow. Run it in a high-availability configuration (at least two replicas in a gossip cluster) so that a single instance failure does not suppress alerts during incidents.

Prometheus's local TSDB is not replicated by default. Use remote-write to a durable backend (Thanos, Cortex, or a managed service) or periodically snapshot the TSDB to object storage so that historical metric data survives infrastructure failures.

alert that fires if no metrics have been received from the agent for more than 2 minutes. This detects a broken instrumentation pipeline — a scenario where everything looks green because no data is arriving, rather than because the system is actually healthy. The OTEL SDK exposes a
metric that can serve this purpose.
Limit metric fan-out from the Collector.

Sending every metric to every backend multiplies ingest costs. Use the Collector's routing processor to send cost-related metrics only to the billing dashboard data source, and send all metrics to Prometheus only for the operational team.

The core metric categories for agentic AI are LLM call latency and token counts, tool execution success/failure rates, agent loop depth per task, task completion status, and cost per unit time — all must be tracked before moving to production.
Use OpenTelemetry counters and histograms at module level; record latency in a
block to capture errors and timeouts in the distribution, not just successes.
Keep metric labels low-cardinality (model, status, tool_name, agent_id); push high-cardinality identifiers like user_id and task_id into traces and logs where they belong.

The OTel Collector acts as a security and routing boundary — use it to redact PII from spans, apply tail-based sampling for traces, and fan out to multiple backends from a single instrumented codebase.
Grafana's ability to correlate Prometheus metrics, Tempo traces, and Loki logs in a single dashboard is the fastest path from "something is wrong" to root cause — set it up before you need it.

Alert on burn rate (multi-window) rather than raw threshold crossing to reduce false positives from transient spikes; include runbook links in alert annotations so on-call engineers can act within seconds of receiving a notification.

Metrics measure performance and availability but not quality; complement the metrics pipeline with evaluation harnesses that assess whether the agent's outputs are factually correct and useful, since a perfectly healthy metric dashboard does not guarantee good answers.
